aspects.name: Simple probability from counts
aspects.description: Compute P(event) = favorable/total when both the favorable count and total count are directly given or trivially computed from a list, written description, or one-category frequency table. The student performs a single division (no row/column conditioning, no reverse algebra).
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A1 differs from A2 in that A1 never requires reading a two-way (joint) table; A1 differs from A3 in that A1 reports an unconditional probability over the whole sample, with no 'given that ...' restriction; A1 differs from A4 in that A1 gives the counts and asks for the probability, not the reverse.

types.name: List or text-described count
types.description: Stem describes a finite collection in prose (or a short list) — a bag of marbles, items in a shipment, planets, houses, a labeled die, members of a group — and asks for the probability of selecting an item with a stated property.
types.core_skill_demand: Identify favorable count k and total count n from the prose, then output k/n in the form requested (fraction, decimal, or symbolic in terms of n).

characteristics: total is small (3-100) and stated explicitly
favorable count is one digit and stated directly ('4 of which were defective', '11 members who play saxophone')
answer is a clean fraction or decimal that matches a choice exactly
no rearrangement or counting of categories needed
easy.example_stem: A bag has 20 buttons: 8 white, 2 orange, 10 brown. Probability of selecting a white button?

characteristics: favorable count is a single category with a less-common label, requiring careful reading (e.g., letter D at one of 14 vertices)
answer is required as a decimal/fraction (SPR) rather than chosen from options
total may be larger (>50) or requires light arithmetic to confirm
medium.example_stem: A 14-sided polygon has vertices labeled A through N. What is the probability that vertex D is selected?

characteristics: does not appear at hard level for this type — pure single-count probability questions cap at medium

must_preserve: favorable and total counts directly determinable without joint-table reasoning
answer form k/n where k, n come from the prose
must_vary: object domain (marbles, buttons, books, planets, animals, students, dice faces)
surface descriptors of the favorable category
answer format (fraction vs decimal vs symbolic-in-n)
common_bugs: computing P(not event) instead of P(event) by listing the complement category
using a wrong total (e.g., summing only some categories)
treating a 'k of every n' rate as k/(n-k) instead of k/n
outputting a percent when fraction/decimal is required (esp. on SPR)

types.name: One-category frequency table
types.description: Stem provides a one-way frequency table (Category | Frequency) and asks for the probability of selecting an item in a stated category. Total is given in the table or stated in the prose.
types.core_skill_demand: Read the favorable frequency from the table row, divide by the stated/summed total, and report as a fraction or decimal.

characteristics: 3-5 category rows
totals row provided or total stated in prose
answer fraction simplifies cleanly or matches a decimal choice (e.g., 10/25 = 0.40)
favorable category is named verbatim in the table
easy.example_stem: Marbles in a bag: Red 8, Blue 10, Green 22, Total 40. P(blue)?

characteristics: not observed at medium — one-way table simple-probability is consistently easy
not observed at hard

must_preserve: unconditional probability over full table total
common_bugs: adding wrong rows for the favorable count
using a sub-total (e.g., excluding the favorable row) as the denominator
selecting the complement probability (1 - P) when 'not X' wording appears

types.name: Multi-category compound count
types.description: Stem lists counts for several categories and asks for the probability of an event that spans multiple categories (e.g., 'neither X nor Y', 'X or Y').
types.core_skill_demand: Sum the relevant favorable counts across categories, divide by the total of all categories, and simplify.

characteristics: not observed at easy
4 named categories with counts, total not directly given (must be summed)
favorable region defined by 'neither A nor B' or 'A or B' wording, requiring a union/complement step
counts contain a distractor cluster (e.g., two equal large counts) that tempts mis-summing
medium.example_stem: Bag has 7 red, 4 white, 33 blue, 33 yellow cubes. P(neither blue nor yellow)?

characteristics: not observed at hard for the prose-list form (hard compound questions migrate to A5 or A2)

must_preserve: multiple named categories with explicit counts
favorable event defined by union or complement across categories
must_vary: which categories form the favorable set
phrasing ('neither X nor Y' vs 'X or Y' vs 'not X')
common_bugs: taking the union as the sum without checking 'neither' = complement
summing all four categories as favorable (treating 'neither' as 'any')
forgetting to compute the total denominator from all rows

aspects.name: Two-way table — joint or marginal probability
aspects.description: Compute an unconditional probability over the whole sample using a two-way (cross-classified) table. The favorable region is a single cell (joint) or a single row/column total (marginal); the denominator is the grand total.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A2 differs from A3 in that A2's denominator is the grand total, with no 'given that' restriction; A3 restricts the denominator to a row or column subtotal. A2 differs from A1 in requiring a two-way table — collapsing the table to one dimension is categorical drift toward A1.

types.name: Marginal probability from two-way table
types.description: Stem presents a two-way table and asks for the probability of a single row-or-column-defined property over the grand total (e.g., 'P(at least 40 years old)', 'P(talks on cell phone daily)').
types.core_skill_demand: Identify the row or column marginal total for the favorable property, divide by the grand total.

characteristics: table includes Total row and column already computed
favorable property is one entire row or column (marginal)
answer is a fraction with the grand total in the denominator and matches a choice
easy.example_stem: Members of an organization classified by age (<40 / ≥40) × side of river. P(at least 40)?

characteristics: not observed — marginal-from-2x2 stays easy
not observed

must_preserve: two-way table with both totals supplied
favorable region is a row marginal or column marginal
must_vary: context (age × location, behavior × demographic, purchase × purchase)
common_bugs: picking a single cell instead of the row/column total
dividing by a row/column subtotal instead of the grand total (this is the A3 drift)
swapping favorable and complement rows

types.name: Joint cell probability
types.description: Stem asks for the probability of an item satisfying two properties simultaneously (one cell of a two-way table), with denominator equal to the grand total.
types.core_skill_demand: Locate the single cell at the intersection of the two stated properties, divide by the grand total.

characteristics: context narrows favorable region by year/group already stated
denominator equals a sub-total that is explicitly labeled (so it feels like marginal reading)
easy.example_stem: Of students who interned in 2010, what fraction were from Valley HS?

characteristics: two simultaneous conditions, both surface ('hybrid AND priced ≤ $25,000', 'has camping AND no bicycle paths')
totals row and column present but the student must resist using either subtotal as the denominator
table is 2×2 with a small grand total (12-14) and the joint cell is small (e.g., 2/14, 5/37)
not observed at hard — joint-cell questions don't escalate; hard variants migrate into A3 (conditional)

must_preserve: two simultaneous conditions defining the cell
denominator is the grand total of the table
common_bugs: dividing the cell by a row or column subtotal (drifts to A3 conditional)
summing both qualifying-row cells when only the intersection cell is favorable
using the favorable row total instead of the joint cell as numerator

types.name: Read a single count from a two-way table
types.description: Stem asks for a count (not a probability) read directly from a two-way table cell — typically as a setup or simpler variant of the joint-cell idea.
types.core_skill_demand: Locate the cell described by the two stated conditions and report its count.

characteristics: question asks 'how many' not 'what probability'
table is described in prose with explicit row/column labels
single cell lookup
easy.example_stem: Of 1,000 students surveyed (sport × gender), how many males do not play a school sport?

characteristics: not observed
not observed

common_bugs: reporting a probability when a raw count is requested
selecting the wrong cell when row/column labels are similar

types.name: Marginal probability with SPR output
types.description: Same as T4 (marginal from two-way table) but the table layout is more elaborate (3+ row categories or 3+ column categories) and the answer must be entered as a decimal/fraction.
types.core_skill_demand: Sum the favorable row or column total, divide by grand total, write as decimal or simplified fraction.

characteristics: not observed at easy
3-column or 3-row layout (e.g., 2×3 color × shape table)
SPR answer entry (no choices to anchor the form)
the marginal asked-for is one full row or column
medium.example_stem: Color (R/B/Y) × Shape (Square/Pentagon) for 100 tiles. P(red)? SPR.

characteristics: not observed

must_preserve: two-way table with at least one dimension having 3+ categories
common_bugs: entering as percent when fraction/decimal required
dropping a category when summing the marginal
using a cell instead of the marginal total

aspects.name: Conditional probability (given subset)
aspects.description: Compute P(A | B) where the 'given' restricts the denominator to a row, column, or other stated subset of the sample. The numerator is the count in the intersection of the favorable property with the conditioning subset.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A3 differs from A2 in that the denominator is a row/column subtotal (or filtered subset), not the grand total; clones that divide by the grand total are categorical drift toward A2.

types.name: Conditional from two-way table — row/column given
types.description: Stem presents a two-way table and asks 'P(A | B)?' or 'Of [subset B], what fraction/probability is A?' — the denominator is the subtotal for B.
types.core_skill_demand: Locate the cell at A ∩ B and divide by the marginal total for B (not the grand total).

characteristics: not observed at easy — conditional reading from a two-way table is always medium+
table is 2×2 or has clearly labeled totals
conditioning subset is one explicit row or column (e.g., 'works in HR', 'chocolate-colored kittens', 'no-rain days')
the two cells in the conditioning subset are small integers; division produces a clean fraction
medium.example_stem: HR has 4 BA + 2 MA; given the chosen person works in HR, P(MA)?

characteristics: conditioning subset is the union of two or more rows/columns ('at least 10 years of age' = '10-19' ∪ '20+', 'rhesus-negative' = one row), forcing the student to sum cells for both the numerator and denominator
table is 3×3 or larger so the favorable cell is not in the same simple position as the subtotal label
answer requested as a non-percent decimal or fraction with SPR entry, or as a percent with tenth-of-a-percent precision
or: conditional appears inside a reverse setup (given a probability, find a missing cell — see T9 hard)
hard.example_stem: Group × Age (3×3, 90 total). P(Group A | age ≥ 10)? = (cells in A across 10-19 and 20+) / (total across 10-19 and 20+).

must_preserve: denominator equals a row/column subtotal or a sum of such subtotals
must_vary: which dimension is the conditioning subset
whether the conditioning subset is a single row/column or a union
common_bugs: dividing by the grand total instead of the subset total (drift to A2)
swapping numerator and denominator so reporting P(B|A) instead of P(A|B)
when 'given' spans a union (e.g., 'at least 10 years old'), forgetting to sum two columns
reporting count instead of probability
outputting percent on SPR where decimal/fraction required

types.name: Conditional from rate/structure (no table)
types.description: Stem describes a structured population in prose (rows × items per row, with sub-counts per row) and asks for a conditional probability — student must mentally build the cross-classification.
types.core_skill_demand: Compute counts for each cell of the implied two-way table from the multiplicative prose ('6 rows × 8 tall + 6 rows × 6 short' …), identify the conditioning subset, and divide.

characteristics: not observed
not observed
no table given — student must compute four products and group them
two factors per category (rows of trees × trees per row, split by tall/short)
conditional phrasing ('given that the tree is 20 feet or taller') requires re-grouping by the conditioning property
hard.example_stem: 6 rows birch (8 tall, 6 short per row); 5 rows maple (9 tall, 7 short per row). Given tree ≥ 20 ft, P(maple)?

must_preserve: population described in multiplicative prose (rows × items, batches × per-batch counts, etc.)
must_vary: which dimension is asked vs given
the multiplicative structure (rows × items vs batches × units)
common_bugs: forgetting to multiply rows × per-row before classifying
using the grand total as denominator instead of the conditioning subtotal
only summing the favorable group without subtracting/intersecting with the conditioning property

types.name: Reverse conditional — solve for missing cell
types.description: Stem presents a two-way table with one unknown cell (x), states a conditional probability (e.g., 'P(B | rhesus-negative) = 1/9'), and asks the student to solve for x or for a derived quantity.
types.core_skill_demand: Express the stated conditional as cell/subtotal = stated probability, solve algebraically for the unknown cell, then answer the actual question (often a count or another probability).

characteristics: not observed
not observed
table has an unknown cell labeled x
stated conditional involves a subtotal that itself depends on x (denominator = 7 + 2 + 1 + x)
answer may require an additional reverse step beyond solving for x
hard.example_stem: Blood type × rhesus table with rhesus-negative row [7, 2, 1, x]; P(B | rhesus-negative) = 1/9 → find x.

must_preserve: stated conditional probability anchoring the algebra
common_bugs: treating denominator as fixed (ignoring that subtotal includes x)
solving for the wrong cell
stopping at x when the question wants a different quantity

aspects.name: Reverse — given probability, find count or total
aspects.description: The probability (or several probabilities summing to a constraint) is given and the student must recover a count, a total, or a missing parameter. Direction is reversed compared to A1/A2: P is input, count/total is output.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A4 differs from A1/A2/A3 in the direction of inference: instead of dividing counts to get a probability, the student multiplies (or solves) using a given probability to recover a count. A clone that ends up asking for P drifts categorically into A1.

types.name: P × total = count
types.description: Stem gives a total population and a probability; asks for the count of the favorable category.
types.core_skill_demand: Multiply the total by the probability to recover the favorable count.

characteristics: total is a clean integer (e.g., 60)
probability is a one- or two-decimal value (e.g., 0.35)
product is an integer matching a choice
easy.example_stem: Bag of 60 marbles, P(blue) = 0.35. How many blue marbles?

characteristics: not observed at medium for this exact pattern (medium reverse problems move to T12)
not observed

must_preserve: total given, probability given, count asked
common_bugs: subtracting probability from total (mishandling decimal as count)
reporting the complement count
dividing instead of multiplying

types.name: Three-category constraint: probabilities sum to 1
types.description: Stem gives a total and two of three probabilities; asks for the count in the third category.
types.core_skill_demand: Compute P(third) = 1 − P(A) − P(B), then multiply by total to get the count.

characteristics: not observed at easy — two-step reverse pushes it to medium
three mutually-exclusive categories (A, B, C)
two probabilities given as decimals; sum requires a complement-to-1 step
total is a clean three-digit integer (e.g., 350, 275) so final product is an integer
medium.example_stem: 350 customers across theaters A, B, C with P(A)=0.48, P(B)=0.24. Count in C?

characteristics: not observed at hard for this structure (hard reverse problems migrate to T13)

must_preserve: two probabilities supplied, third inferred via 1 − P1 − P2
common_bugs: multiplying total by (P1 + P2) instead of by (1 − P1 − P2)
reporting probability instead of count
stopping at P(C) and forgetting the multiplication

types.name: Multi-step reverse with growth/change
types.description: Stem describes a changing population (initial total → later total), gives a probability at the later time, and asks for a count (or rounded count) within the later population.
types.core_skill_demand: Identify the relevant later-time total, multiply by the given probability, round as instructed.

characteristics: not observed
not observed
population changes between two stated dates so two totals are present; only one is the correct denominator
probability is a decimal (e.g., 0.43) and product is non-integer requiring rounding
context is dense (multiple providers/categories mentioned) creating distractor totals
SPR answer entry
hard.example_stem: May: 83M subscribers; Sept: 97M. P(MTN on Sept) = 0.43. Find p (millions of MTN, Sept) to nearest integer.

must_preserve: given probability tied to one specific time/condition
must_vary: rounding precision (nearest integer vs nearest tenth)
common_bugs: using the wrong (earlier) total as the denominator
forgetting the rounding step or rounding incorrectly
treating 'p million' literally — outputting decimal millions instead of the requested integer

aspects.name: Compound or abstract probability
aspects.description: Probability questions whose favorable region is defined by a combination of conditions on an abstract set (integers, fractions of a stream), not a categorical table or list. Often requires counting integers in a constrained range or computing a ratio of cumulative quantities.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A5 differs from A1 in that the sample is abstract (integers, time intervals) rather than a tangible list of items; differs from A2/A3 in that no two-way table is involved; differs from A4 in that a probability is reported, not a count.

types.name: Intersection on an integer set
types.description: Stem defines a set of integers (e.g., positive integers ≤ N) and asks for the probability that a randomly chosen integer satisfies two or more conditions (e.g., 'even AND ≤ 50').
types.core_skill_demand: Count the integers in the intersection of the conditions, divide by the size of the underlying set.

characteristics: not observed
not observed
set is large (N ≥ 100) so counting is non-trivial
favorable region is an intersection ('even AND ≤ 50') requiring two-condition counting
answer reported as fraction or decimal (no choices to confirm)

must_preserve: favorable region defined by two intersecting numeric conditions
common_bugs: counting one condition only (e.g., all evens) and ignoring the second
off-by-one when the bound is inclusive ('≤ 50' includes 50)
using wrong denominator (e.g., evens in set instead of size of set)

types.name: Rate-based fraction of a stream
types.description: Stem describes a constant arrival/registration rate per unit time and asks what fraction of a larger total accrues in a smaller time window.
types.core_skill_demand: Compute the count for the smaller window (rate × time), divide by the larger total, simplify.

characteristics: not observed at easy — this rate-style framing skips easy
rate stated per day (or per unit)
two totals involved (rate × small window vs. given large total) — student must keep them distinct
answer is a simple fraction (e.g., 1/10)
medium.example_stem: 1,200 registrations/day. What fraction of first 60,000 are registered in the first 5 days?

characteristics: not observed

common_bugs: dividing rate by total without multiplying by window length
using window length as the denominator
reporting an absolute count rather than a fraction

types.name: Compare sub-marginal to a target fraction
types.description: Stem presents a one-way or two-way table and asks which category's count is closest to a specified fraction (e.g., one-third) of a marginal total.
types.core_skill_demand: Compute the target value (fraction × marginal), then identify which category's count is closest.

characteristics: not observed at easy
target is a simple fraction (1/3, 1/2) of a marginal total
candidate categories produce close counts so the student cannot eyeball — must compute
may include a conditional restriction ('Of those who were at least 50 years old, what fraction were at least 60?')
medium.example_stem: Penguin table by type × gender, total female = 180. Which type's female count is closest to one-third of 180?

characteristics: not observed

must_vary: whether the marginal is computed or stated
common_bugs: using the grand total instead of the relevant marginal
comparing raw counts to the fraction instead of to fraction × marginal
selecting the largest category (mistaking 'closest to one-third' for 'biggest')

skill_wide_notes: Easy items cluster in A1 (single-step counting) and A2/T4 (two-way marginal reading). Medium items introduce a complement-to-1 step (A4/T12), a joint cell with distractor subtotals (A2/T5), or the first conditional reading (A3/T8 medium). Hard items overwhelmingly live in A3 (conditional reading where the 'given' is a union of rows/columns, or a reverse conditional that solves for a missing cell) and in A5 (abstract integer sets, rate-based streams). Difficulty levers across the skill: (i) whether denominator is the grand total (easy) vs a single row/column subtotal (medium) vs a union of row/column subtotals (hard); (ii) whether counts are stated explicitly (easy) or must be assembled from prose multiplication (hard, A3/T9); (iii) whether the direction is forward (counts → P, easy/medium) or reverse (P → count, medium/hard); (iv) SPR vs MCQ — SPR variants tend to be one notch harder because the student cannot back-solve from choices.
